content: A Carnot engine has an efficiency of $\frac{1}{6}$. When the temperature of sink is reduced by 62 K , its efficiency is doubled. The temperature of the source is
explanation:
Given,

$$ \eta_1=\frac{1}{6}=1-\frac{\mathrm{T}_2}{\mathrm{~T}_1}=\Rightarrow \mathrm{T}_2=\frac{5}{6} \mathrm{~T}_1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

Also,

$$ \eta_2=\frac{1}{3}=1-\frac{\left(T_2-62\right)}{T_1} $$

Substituting (i),

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \frac{\left(\frac{5}{6} \mathrm{~T}_1-62\right)}{\mathrm{T}_1}=1-\frac{1}{3} \\ & \frac{5}{6} \mathrm{~T}_1-62=\mathrm{T}_1 \frac{2}{3} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{~T}_1=372 \mathrm{~K} \\ \therefore & \mathrm{~T}_1=372-273=99^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \end{array} $$

content: If the r.m.s. velocity of gas is V at temperature T , then
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text { R.M.S. velocity is given by }\\ &\begin{aligned} V & =\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}} \\ V^2 & =\frac{3 R T}{M} \\ \frac{V^2}{T} & =\text { constant } \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

content: In resistance thermometer, the resistance at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are $6.74 \Omega$ and $7.74 \Omega$ respectively. The temperature corresponding to $6.53 \Omega$ resistance is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{T} & =\frac{\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{R}_0}{\mathrm{R}_{100}-\mathrm{R}_0} \times 100 \\ \therefore \quad \mathrm{~T} & =\frac{6.53-6.74}{7.74-6.74} \times 100=\frac{-0.21}{1} \times 100=-21^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \end{aligned} $$

content: An ideal gas in a container of volume 500 cc is at a pressure of $2 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$. The average kinetic energy of each molecule is $6 \times 10^{-21} \mathrm{~J}$. The number of gas molecules in the container is
explanation:
Let's restate what we’re given:

  • Volume $ V = 500 \, \mathrm{cc} = 500 \times 10^{-6} \, \mathrm{m^3} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \, \mathrm{m^3} $

  • Pressure $ P = 2 \times 10^5 \, \mathrm{N/m^2} $

  • Average kinetic energy per molecule $ E = 6 \times 10^{-21}\, \mathrm{J} $

  • We are to find the number of molecules $ N $.

Step 1: Recall the ideal gas relation in terms of molecular quantities

For an ideal gas,

$ PV = \frac{2}{3} N \left(\text{average kinetic energy per molecule}\right) $

That is,

$ P V = \frac{2}{3} N E $

Step 2: Solve for $ N $

$ N = \frac{3PV}{2E} $

Step 3: Substitute values

$ N = \frac{3 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times (5 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \times (6 \times 10^{-21})} $

Compute step by step:

Numerator:

$ 3 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 3 \times 10^5 \times 10^{-3} = 3 \times 10^2 = 300 $

Wait, let’s carefully compute:

$ 3 \times (2 \times 10^5) \times (5 \times 10^{-4}) = 3 \times 2 \times 5 \times 10^{5 - 4} = 30 \times 10^1 = 300 $

✅ Numerator = 300

Denominator:

$ 2 \times (6 \times 10^{-21}) = 12 \times 10^{-21} = 1.2 \times 10^{-20} $

So,

$ N = \frac{300}{1.2 \times 10^{-20}} = 2.5 \times 10^{22} $

✅ Final Answer:

$ \boxed{N = 2.5 \times 10^{22}} $

Correct Option: D

content: In thermodynamic process, for free expansion, out of the following select the 'WRONG' statement.

options.content: Free expansion are adiabatic expansions and there is no exchange of heat between a system and its environment.
There is no work done on the system or by the system.
It is an instantaneous change, and the system is not in thermodynamic equilibrium
A free expansion can be plotted on a p-v diagram.

explanation:
Concept Recap:

  • In free expansion, a gas expands into vacuum (no opposing external pressure).

  • It happens adiabatically (no heat exchange, $ Q = 0 $),

    and without work, since $ W = \int P_{ext} dV = 0 $.

  • It is a non-quasi-static process — not in equilibrium during expansion.

  • Because it’s non-equilibrium, it cannot be represented on a single P–V curve, since intermediate states are undefined.

Option Analysis:

Option A:

Free expansions are adiabatic expansions and there is no exchange of heat between a system and its environment.

✅ Correct, because $ Q = 0 $.

Option B:

There is no work done on the system or by the system.

✅ Correct, since expansion is against vacuum, $ P_{ext} = 0 \Rightarrow W = 0 $.

Option C:

It is an instantaneous change, and the system is not in thermodynamic equilibrium.

✅ Correct, since the process is irreversible and no intermediate equilibrium states exist.

Option D:

A free expansion can be plotted on a p–v diagram.

❌ Wrong, because the process is non-quasi-static; intermediate $P$ values are undefined, so it cannot be properly plotted on a P–V diagram.

✅ Correct Answer: Option D

content: The heat required to increase the temperature from 373 K to 573 K at constant volume for ideal monoatomic gas of 2 moles is ( $R=$ universal gas constant)
explanation:
For monoatomic gas at constant volume:

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{nC}_{\mathrm{v}} \Delta \mathrm{~T} \\ & \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}=\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{R} \end{aligned} $$

Given:

$$ \begin{aligned} & n=2 \\ & \Delta T=573-373=200 K \\ & Q=2 \cdot \frac{3}{2} R \cdot 200=3 R \cdot 200=600 R \end{aligned} $$

content: A gas at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is cooled until both its pressure and volume are halved. The final temperature of the gas will be
When the r.m.s. velocity of a gas is denoted by ' V ' and temperature of the gas by T , which of the following relations is TRUE?
explanation:
Let's recall the relation for the r.m.s. velocity of a gas:

$ v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{3kT}{m}} $

where

  • $k$ = Boltzmann constant (or $R$ for molar form)

  • $T$ = absolute temperature

  • $m$ = mass of one molecule (or $M$ = molar mass)

Step 1: Express relation between $V$ and $T$

$ V \propto \sqrt{T} $

Step 2: Square both sides

$ V^2 \propto T $

$ \frac{V^2}{T} = \text{constant} $

✅ Correct Relation:

$ \boxed{\frac{V^2}{T} = \text{constant}} $

✅ Answer: Option C

content: The average kinetic energy of a molecule of the gas is

options.content: dependent on the nature of the gas.

explanation:
The correct answer is:

✅ Option C: proportional to absolute temperature.

Explanation:

From the kinetic theory of gases, the average kinetic energy of a molecule is given by

$ \text{Average kinetic energy} = \frac{3}{2} k T $

where

  • $ k $ is the Boltzmann constant, and

  • $ T $ is the absolute temperature (in kelvins).

Thus, the average kinetic energy depends only on the absolute temperature, not on pressure, volume, or the nature of the gas.

content: Two spherical black bodies of radius $\mathrm{R}_1$ and $\mathrm{R}_2$ with surface temperatures $\mathrm{T}_1$ and $\mathrm{T}_2$ respectively. If $T_1=2 T_2$ and they radiate the same power then relation between $R_1$ and $R_2$ is
explanation:
For any two bodies of radii $\mathrm{R}_1$ and $\mathrm{R}_2$, kept at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, the power radiated or rate of loss of heat by them can be given

$$ \text { as, } \frac{Q_1}{Q_2}=\left(\frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)^2 \times\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right)^4 $$

$\therefore \quad$ For same power,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(\frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)^2=\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^4 \\ \therefore & \left(\frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)=\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^2=\left(\frac{T_2}{2 T_2}\right)^2=\frac{1}{4} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & R_2=4 R_1 \end{aligned} $$

content: In an adiabatic change, the pressure and temperature of a diatomic gas are related as $\mathrm{P} \propto \mathrm{T}^{\mathrm{c}}$, where c is a constant. The value of c is (consider gas molecules are rigid)
explanation:
Given

For an adiabatic process of an ideal gas:

$ P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant} $

where $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$.

For a diatomic gas (rigid)

A rigid diatomic molecule means no vibrational degrees of freedom are active.

So degrees of freedom $f = 5$ (3 translational + 2 rotational).

Therefore,

$ C_v = \frac{f}{2}R = \frac{5}{2}R, \quad C_p = C_v + R = \frac{7}{2}R $

and

$ \gamma = \frac{C_p}{C_v} = \frac{7/2}{5/2} = \frac{7}{5} $

Relating $P$ and $T$

We know:

$ P V = nRT \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac{nRT}{P} $

Substitute this into $P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$:

$ P \left(\frac{nRT}{P}\right)^{\gamma} = \text{constant} $

Simplify:

$ P^{1 - \gamma} T^{\gamma} = \text{constant} $

Hence,

$ P^{1 - \gamma} \propto T^{-\gamma} $

or, rearranging for $P$ in terms of $T$:

$ P \propto T^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1}} $

Substitute $\gamma = \tfrac{7}{5}$

$ c = \frac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{7/5}{(7/5) - 1} = \frac{7/5}{2/5} = \frac{7}{2} = 3.5 $

✅ Answer:

$ \boxed{c = \frac{7}{2}} $

Correct Option: (B) $ \dfrac{7}{2} $

content: A system is compressed adiabatically, then its temperature
explanation:
In an adiabatic compression, no heat is exchanged with the surroundings ($ Q = 0 $).

The work done on the gas increases its internal energy.

Since internal energy is directly related to temperature ($ \Delta U = n C_v \Delta T $), the temperature increases.

✅ Correct Answer: Option A — increases.

content: A blackbody at $1227^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ emits radiation with maximum intensity at a wavelength of $5600 $$\mathop {\rm{A}}\limits^{\rm{o}} $. If the temperature of the body is increased by $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the maximum intensity will be at wavelength
explanation:
Given:

  • Initial temperature, $T_1 = 1227^\circ \mathrm{C} = 1227 + 273 = 1500\,\mathrm{K}$

  • Initial wavelength of maximum intensity, $\lambda_1 = 5600\,\mathrm{\mathring{A}}$

  • Temperature is increased by $1000^\circ \mathrm{C}$

Step 1: Find the new temperature:

$$ T_2 = (1227 + 1000)^\circ \mathrm{C} = 2227^\circ \mathrm{C} = 2227 + 273 = 2500\,\mathrm{K} $$

Step 2: Use Wien's Displacement Law

Wien's Law states:

$$ \lambda_\text{max} T = \text{constant} $$

Step 3: Calculate new wavelength of maximum intensity, $\lambda_2$:

$$ \lambda_1 T_1 = \lambda_2 T_2 $$

So,

$$ \lambda_2 = \frac{\lambda_1 T_1}{T_2} $$

Substitute the values:

$$ \lambda_2 = \frac{5600 \times 1500}{2500} $$

Calculate:

$$ 5600 \times 1500 = 8\,400\,000 $$

$$ \lambda_2 = \frac{8\,400\,000}{2500} = 3360\,\mathrm{\mathring{A}} $$

Final Answer:

Option B: $3360\,\mathrm{\mathring{A}}$

content: An ideal gas of mass ' $M$ ' is in the state ' $A$ ' goes to another state B via three different processes. If $Q_1, Q_2$ and $Q_3$ denote the heat absorbed by the gas along the paths 1,2 and 3 respectively, then
explanation:
As the inertial and final states are the same, $\Delta \mathrm{U}=0$.

From first law of thermodynamics, $\Delta Q=\Delta W$

$\Delta \mathrm{W}$ is given by the area enclosed by the curve.

$$ \therefore \quad W_1 < W_2 < W_3 \Rightarrow Q_1 < Q_2 < Q_3 $$

content: An ideal gas is heated from $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $627^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at constant pressure. If initial volume of gas is $4 \mathrm{~m}^3$, then the final volume of the gas will be
explanation:
Given:

Initial temperature, $T_1 = 27^\circ \mathrm{C}$

Final temperature, $T_2 = 627^\circ \mathrm{C}$

Initial volume, $V_1 = 4~\mathrm{m}^3$

Process: Constant pressure

Find: Final volume, $V_2$

Step 1: Convert temperatures to Kelvin

$ T_1 = 27^\circ \mathrm{C} + 273 = 300~\mathrm{K} $

$ T_2 = 627^\circ \mathrm{C} + 273 = 900~\mathrm{K} $

Step 2: Use the ideal gas law (at constant pressure)

If pressure is constant, then

$ \frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2} $

Step 3: Substitute the values

$ \frac{4}{300} = \frac{V_2}{900} $

Step 4: Solve for $V_2$

$ V_2 = \frac{900 \times 4}{300} $

$ V_2 = \frac{3600}{300} $

$ V_2 = 12~\mathrm{m}^3 $

Final Answer:

$\boxed{12~\mathrm{m}^3}$ (Option D)

content: Pressure of the gas remaining same, the temperature at which r. m. s. speed of the gas molecules is double its value at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is
explanation:
Let the initial temperature $T_1 = 27^\circ \mathrm{C} = 300\,\mathrm{K}$.

Let the initial root mean square (r.m.s.) speed be $u_1$, and the final r.m.s. speed be $u_2 = 2u_1$.

At constant pressure $P$, r.m.s. speed is given by:

$$ u = \sqrt{\frac{3RT}{M}} $$

where $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin.

Step 1: Write the relation for r.m.s. speeds at two temperatures,

$$ \frac{u_2}{u_1} = \sqrt{\frac{T_2}{T_1}} $$

Given $u_2 = 2u_1$, so

$$ \frac{2u_1}{u_1} = \sqrt{\frac{T_2}{T_1}} \implies 2 = \sqrt{\frac{T_2}{T_1}} $$

Step 2: Square both sides,

$$ 2^2 = \frac{T_2}{T_1} \implies 4 = \frac{T_2}{T_1} $$

Step 3: Find $T_2$,

$$ T_2 = 4 T_1 = 4 \times 300 = 1200\,\mathrm{K} $$

Step 4: Convert $T_2$ back to Celsius,

$$ T (^ \circ C) = T(\mathrm{K}) - 273\\ T_2 (^ \circ C) = 1200 - 273 = 927^\circ \mathrm{C} $$

Correct option: Option B, $927^\circ \mathrm{C}$

content: Rate of flow of heat through a cylindrical rod is $\mathrm{H}_1$. The temperature of the ends of the rod are ' $\mathrm{T}_1$ ' and ' $\mathrm{T}_2$ '. If all the dimensions of the rod become double and the temperature difference remains the same, the rate of flow of heat becomes ' $\mathrm{H}_2$ '. Then $\mathrm{H}_2=$
explanation:
The rate of flow of heat through a cylindrical rod (using the formula according to NCERT):

$ H = \frac{kA (T_1 - T_2)}{L} $

where

  • $H$ = rate of flow of heat

  • $k$ = thermal conductivity of the material

  • $A$ = cross-sectional area of the rod

  • $L$ = length of the rod

  • $T_1$ and $T_2$ = temperatures at the two ends

Let the original rod have:

  • Length = $L$

  • Radius = $r$

  • Cross-sectional area, $A = \pi r^2$

So,

$ H_1 = \frac{k\pi r^2 (T_1 - T_2)}{L} $

When all dimensions are doubled:

  • New length, $L' = 2L$

  • New radius, $r' = 2r$

  • New area, $A' = \pi (2r)^2 = 4\pi r^2$

Now,

$ H_2 = \frac{kA'(T_1 - T_2)}{L'} = \frac{k \cdot 4\pi r^2 (T_1 - T_2)}{2L} = 2 \cdot \frac{k \pi r^2 (T_1 - T_2)}{L} = 2H_1 $

So, the correct answer is:

$ \boxed{2H_1} $

This is Option B.

content: For which of the following combinations of working temperatures, the efficiency of Carnot's engine is maximum?
explanation:
The efficiency $\eta$ of a Carnot engine working between temperatures $T_1$ (hot reservoir) and $T_2$ (cold reservoir), both in Kelvin, is given by:

$$ \eta = 1 - \frac{T_2}{T_1} $$

Let's calculate the efficiency for each option:

Option A: $T_1 = 40\,\text{K},\ T_2 = 20\,\text{K}$

$$ \eta_A = 1 - \frac{20}{40} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 $$

Option B: $T_1 = 50\,\text{K},\ T_2 = 30\,\text{K}$

$$ \eta_B = 1 - \frac{30}{50} = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4 $$

Option C: $T_1 = 70\,\text{K},\ T_2 = 50\,\text{K}$

$$ \eta_C = 1 - \frac{50}{70} = 1 - 0.714 = 0.286 $$

Option D: $T_1 = 90\,\text{K},\ T_2 = 60\,\text{K}$

$$ \eta_D = 1 - \frac{60}{90} = 1 - 0.667 = 0.333 $$

Comparison:

  • Option A: $0.5$

  • Option B: $0.4$

  • Option C: $0.286$

  • Option D: $0.333$

Maximum efficiency is $0.5$, so the answer is:

Option A: 40 K and 20 K

content:
Two stars 'A' and 'B' radiate maximum energy at $5200 \mathop {\rm{A}}\limits^ \circ$ and $6500 \mathop {\rm{A}}\limits^ \circ$ respectively. Then the ratio of absolute temperatures of stars ' A ' and ' $B$ ' is

$$\mathop {\rm{A}}\limits^ \circ  $$
explanation:
According to Wien’s displacement law,

$$\lambda_{\text{max}}\,T=\text{constant}$$

So for stars A and B,

$$\lambda_A\,T_A=\lambda_B\,T_B\quad\Longrightarrow\quad\frac{T_A}{T_B}=\frac{\lambda_B}{\lambda_A}$$

Given

• $\lambda_A=5200\,$Å

• $\lambda_B=6500\,$Å

Thus

$$\frac{T_A}{T_B}=\frac{6500}{5200}=\frac{65}{52}=\frac{5}{4}$$

Hence the ratio of their absolute temperatures $T_A:T_B$ is

$$5:4$$

Option B.

content: A spherical body of radius ' $r$ ' radiates power ' $P$ ' at $T$ kelvin. If the radius $s$ halved and the temperature doubled the power radiated in the same time ' t ' will be
explanation:
Radiated power by blackbody, $P=\frac{Q}{t}$

For any two bodies of radii $\mathrm{R}_1$ and $\mathrm{R}_2$, kept at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, the power radiated or rate of loss of heat by them can be give as, $\frac{Q_1}{Q_2}=\left(\frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)^2 \times\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right)^4$

In case of non-spherical bodies, $\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right)^2$ can be replaced by $\left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)$ where $A_1, A_2$ are areas of the given bodies.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{P_1}{P_2}=\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^2 \times\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right)^4=\left(\frac{r_1}{\frac{r_1}{2}}\right)^2 \times\left(\frac{T_1}{2 T_1}\right)^4 \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{P_1}{P_2}=4 \times \frac{1}{16} \Rightarrow P_2=4 P_1 \end{aligned} $$

content: In thermodynamic process, which of the following statements is not true?

options.content: In an adiabatic process, the system is insulated from the surroundings.
In an isochoric process, the pressure remains constant.
In an isothermal process, the temperature remains constant.

explanation:
To identify the incorrect statement, recall the definitions of common thermodynamic processes:

Adiabatic process

– Heat exchange with surroundings, $Q = 0$.

– System is insulated ⇒ Option A is true.

– For an ideal gas, $pV^\gamma = \text{constant}$ ⇒ Option D is true.

Isothermal process

– Temperature remains constant, $T = \text{constant}$. ⇒ Option C is true.

Isochoric process

– Volume remains constant, $V = \text{constant}$.

– Since $\Delta V = 0$, work done $W = \int p\,dV = 0$, but pressure can change as temperature or amount of gas changes.

– Pressure does not remain constant ⇒ Option B is false.

Answer: Option B is not true.

content: A monoatomic ideal gas, initially at temperature ' $\mathrm{T}_1$ '' is enclosed in a cylinder fitted with a frictionless piston. The gas is allowed to expand adiabatically to a temperature ' $\mathrm{T}_2$ ' by releasing the piston suddenly. $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the lengths of the gas columns before and after the expansion respectively. Then $\frac{T_1}{T_2}$ is
explanation:
When a gas expands without heat exchange (adiabatically), its temperature and volume satisfy:

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & T_1 V_1^{\gamma-1}=T_2 V_2^{\gamma-1} \\ \therefore & \frac{T_2}{T_1}=\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} \end{array} $$

For a monoatomic gas, $\gamma=\frac{5}{3}$. Then:

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \gamma-1=\frac{5}{3}-1=\frac{2}{3} \\ & V_1=AL_1 \text{ and } V_2=AL_2 \\ \therefore & \frac{T_2}{T_1}=\left[\frac{AL_1}{AL_2}\right]^{2/3}=\left[\frac{L_1}{L_2}\right]^{2/3} \end{array} $$

Since volume is proportional to column length ($V\propto L$), the temperature ratio becomes:

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{T_2}{T_1}=\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1}=\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}} \\ & \text{But }V\propto L \\ \therefore\quad & \frac{T_1}{T_2}=\left(\frac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^{\frac{2}{3}} \end{aligned} $$

content: The volume of a block of metal at $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ changes by $0.12 \%$ when its temperature is increased to $70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is
explanation:
We know the volume change fraction is ΔV/V = 0.12/100 and the temperature change is ΔT = 70 °C − 30 °C = 40 °C.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \gamma=\frac{\Delta V}{V(\Delta T)}=\frac{0.12}{100 \times 40}=3 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\ & \alpha=\frac{\gamma}{3}=1 \times 10^{-5} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \end{aligned} $$

So, the coefficient of linear expansion is α = 1 × 10⁻⁵ /°C.

content: A fixed mass of gas at constant pressure occupies a volume ' $V$ '. The gas undergoes a rise in temperature so that the r.m.s. velocity of the molecule is doubled. The new volume will be
explanation:
Step 1: Write the r.m.s. speed formula.

$$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{3\,RT}{M}} $$

Step 2: Use the ideal gas law $PV = RT$ to replace $RT$.

$$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{3\,P\,V}{M}} $$

Step 3: At constant pressure $P$ and mass $M$, r.m.s. speed depends on volume $V$ as:

$$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}} \propto \sqrt{V} $$

Step 4: Let the initial volume be $V_1$ and final volume be $V_2$. Then

$$ \frac{(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}})_1}{(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}})_2} \;=\;\sqrt{\frac{V_1}{V_2}} $$

Step 5: The problem says the r.m.s. speed doubles, so

$$ (\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}})_2 = 2\,(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{rms}})_1 $$

Substitute this into the ratio:

$$ \sqrt{\frac{V_1}{V_2}} = \frac{1}{2} $$

Step 6: Square both sides and solve for $V_2$:

$$ \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{1}{4} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad V_2 = 4\,V_1 $$
